nativeRange.habitat: Wild base species (A. stuartgranti, A. jacobfreibergi, and related Aulonocara spp.) inhabit the sediment-sand interface zones and rocky-sand transition areas of Lake Malawi, typically along the lake floor where they hunt invertebrates using their lateral-line pit system. OB color-form individuals are captive-bred hybrids and do not occur in the wild.

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (3–5 cm) for natural foraging behaviour
rock piles and caves along the back and sides (cichlid-stack style)
open sand swimming areas in the front
no dense planting required — Vallisneria or Amazon swords tolerated
good surface area and footprint more important than tank height

diet.notes: Primarily invertivore in the wild, using a unique sensory pit system along the lower jaw to detect invertebrates buried in sand. In captivity accepts high-quality cichlid pellets, Spirulina-enriched foods, frozen or freeze-dried brine shrimp, mysis, daphnia, and bloodworms. Avoid mammal-based proteins (beef heart, whole liver) as these can cause Malawi bloat. Feed 1–2 small meals daily; avoid overfeeding.

goodWith: other Aulonocara peacock cichlids (mixed species peacock tank)
Copadichromis and other non-aggressive Utaka haplochromines
Sciaenochromis fryeri (Electric Blue Hap) — with space
Plecos and bottom dwellers that tolerate alkaline water
avoidWith: Mbuna (rock-dwelling cichlids — highly aggressive, will outcompete and harass)
small tetras, rasboras, or other community fish incompatible with rift lake water parameters
other dominant male Aulonocara in small tanks (reduce to 1 male per species per tank)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase image for Aulonocara stuartgranti (wild type, not OB form). Useful for species identification; OB trade fish will differ significantly in coloration.
Wikimedia Commons A. stuartgranti wild type (Flavescent Peacock). Shows base species body shape; OB forms are captive hybrid lines with orange blotch patterning on females.

summary: The OB (Orange Blotch) Peacock is a captive-bred hybrid color form derived from Aulonocara stuartgranti and related species. 'OB' is a trade name, not a wild type — males display vivid iridescent blues/oranges while OB patterning (marbled orange-black) appears primarily in females. Suitable for Lake Malawi community tanks with peaceful haplochromines.
careNotes:
OB Peacock Cichlids are among the most popular of the Malawi peacock/haplochromine cichlids in the aquarium trade, but the name 'OB Peacock' describes a selectively bred or hybridised color morph rather than a distinct wild species. 'OB' stands for Orange Blotch — a color pattern originally found in female Metriaclima (formerly Pseudotropheus) zebra Mbuna and introduced into the Aulonocara lineage through deliberate hybridisation, most commonly with A. stuartgranti or A. jacobfreibergi as the base species. The result is a line where females and occasional males display a vivid marbled orange and black patterning; OB males are rare and prized since the OB gene (carried on the X chromosome) normally only presents in females (XX). Males of OB lines typically display the standard brilliant iridescent blue-yellow-orange peacock coloration of the base species.

Because OB Peacocks are hybrids, their exact scientific designation varies by breeder and lineage; labeling them under A. stuartgranti is a trade convention reflecting the most common base stock, not a strict taxonomic classification. Aquarists interested in preserving wild Aulonocara genetics should seek out species-pure lines from specialist breeders.

Tank setup should replicate a Lake Malawi rocky-sand biotope: hard, alkaline water (pH 7.8–8.6, gH 10–20, kH 8–15, temperature 24–28°C), a fine sand substrate at least 3 cm deep for foraging, and rock stacks along the back providing territory and retreat. A 200-litre tank minimum with 120 cm footprint is recommended; larger is better for managing male aggression. Keep one male per Aulonocara species; multiple males of visually similar species will fight. A ratio of 1 male to 3–5 females per species is optimal. The 'Malawi bloat' disease risk (internal bacterial/parasitic infection linked to high-protein mammal foods) means the diet must emphasise quality cichlid pellets and invertebrate-based frozen foods rather than beef heart. Water changes of 20–30% weekly maintain the buffering capacity and low nitrates critical for rift lake cichlids.
breedingNotes: OB Peacock Cichlids are maternal mouthbrooders. After spawning on open sand (females are attracted to the male's egg-spot-patterned anal fin during the spawning 'dummy egg' display), the female collects and incubates 15–60 eggs orally for approximately 21–28 days at 26°C. Females should not be stripped early unless disease is a concern; natural holding to at least day 18 produces the most developed fry. The female does not feed during mouthbrooding and may become emaciated through multiple broods; ensure she can recover condition between spawns. Fry are released as free-swimming, fully-formed juveniles able to take micro-pellets, finely crushed cichlid food, and brine shrimp nauplii immediately. A separate breeding/grow-out tank reduces fry predation. Sexing juveniles is difficult until males develop adult coloration at approximately 6–12 months. In OB lines, females inheriting the OB gene will display the orange-blotch pattern; the rare OB male (carrying two OB alleles) commands premium prices in the trade.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Peacock Cichlid Care Guide
primary.notes: Primary trade source. Covers tank size (55–75 gal / 208–284 L minimum for community setups), water parameters (pH 7.8–8.5, temperature 76–82°F / 24–28°C), diet (cichlid pellets, frozen invertebrate foods, avoid high-fat mammal proteins), tankmates (peaceful haplochromines, Synodontis; avoid Mbuna), and general peacock cichlid breeding overview. OB as a colour morph/hybrid lineage noted in trade context.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Aulonocara stuartgranti
additional.notes: Detailed wild-species profile for the base species A. stuartgranti. Confirms taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), native range (Lake Malawi, rocky/sandy transition zones), adult male size to 15 cm, water parameters (pH 7.6–8.8, hardness 6–25 dH, temperature 24–26°C), sand-foraging behaviour via lateral-line pit system, maternal mouthbrooding with 21–28 day incubation, and minimum tank 200 L. Notes that commercial 'OB Peacock' lines are hybridised with other Aulonocara and potentially Mbuna strains and do not represent a pure A. stuartgranti lineage.

additional.name: FishBase — Aulonocara stuartgranti
additional.notes: Confirms taxonomy, native range (Lake Malawi, Malawi and Mozambique), adult size (male to 15 cm TL), habitat (benthic, sandy-rocky margins, 3–20 m depth), diet (benthic invertebrates detected by lateral-line), and reproductive strategy (maternal mouthbrooder, substrate spawner). Wild-type species data used to anchor biological parameters for the OB hybrid line.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — OB Peacock Cichlid
additional.notes: Feature article on peacock cichlids including OB colour forms. Confirms OB as a hybrid trade strain where the orange-blotch gene — originally from Mbuna lineage — produces the marbled orange-black colouration predominantly in females; OB males are rare and result from both X chromosomes carrying the OB allele. Recommends keeping peacocks in haplochromine-only or peacock/hap community tanks and emphasises separating from Mbuna. Tank size 200+ L, 1 male per species, 3–5 females per male.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Understanding OB Cichlids
additional.notes: In-depth genetics piece on OB cichlids. Explains that 'OB' (Orange Blotch) is an X-linked dominant colour trait: females (XX) expressing OB are common; OB males (carrying OB allele on one or both X chromosomes relative to Y) are uncommon but occur in some lines. Details the history of OB introduction into the Aulonocara lineage from Metriaclima (Mbuna) hybridisation and discusses the hobbyist debate around OB Peacocks as hybrids vs. selectively bred lines. Recommends clear labelling in the trade to distinguish OB hybrids from wild-type Aulonocara.

fish.breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder; female incubates 15–60 eggs orally for 21–28 days. Spawning triggered by male egg-spot display on sand substrate. Separate breeding tank recommended to protect female and fry. OB patterning in offspring follows X-linked inheritance — OB females common, OB males rare.
